यदा यदा हि धर्मस्य ग्लानिर्भवति भारत ।

sanskrit_iast:
yadā yadā hi dharmasya glānir bhavati bhārata |
abhyutthānam adharmasya tadātmānaṁ sṛjāmy aham || 7 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Whenever and wherever there is a decline in religious practice, O descendant of Bharata, and a predominant rise of irreligion – at that time I descend Myself.
Whenever and wherever there is a decline in religious practice, O descendant of Bharata, and a predominant rise of irreligion—at that time I descend Myself.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 3832 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The word sṛjāmi is significant herein. Sṛjāmi cannot be used in the sense of creation, because, according to the previous verse, there is no creation of the Lord’s form or body, since all of the forms are eternally existent. Therefore, sṛjāmi means that the Lord manifests Himself as He is.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.7 O scion of the Bharata dynasty, yada yada hi, whenever; bhavati, there is; a glanih, decline, decrease; dharmasya, of virtue consisting of the duties of castes and stages of life of living beings, which are the means to achieving properity and Liberation; and abhyutthanam, increase, rise; adharm…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-7-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads dharma here as 'virtue consisting of the duties of castes and stages of life of living beings, which are the means to achieving prosperity and Liberation.' When that declines and adharma rises, the Lord manifests himself through māyā. The mechanism is consistent with 4.6 — appearance, not origination.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 4.7 There is no restriction as to the time of My birth; whenever the Dharma taught by the Vedas that must be observed according to the arrangements of the four stations and the four stages of life declines, and Adharma, its opposite, increases, then I Myself, by My own will and in the manner stated,…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-4-7-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja explicitly says: 'There is no restriction as to the time of My birth.' Whenever the Dharma taught by the Vedas — the dharma of the four stations and stages of life — declines and adharma increases, the Lord by his own will incarnates in the manner stated (4.6). The incarnation is regular, will-governed, real-bodied.

positions.rendering: the duties of castes and stages of life, which are means to prosperity and Liberation
the Dharma taught by the Vedas, observed according to the arrangements of the four stations and four stages

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three commentators read 'dharma' as the structured order of duties prescribed for varṇas (stations) and āśramas (stages). Modern translations often flatten dharma to 'religion' or 'righteousness' but the classical reading is more specific — the structured sociocultural-soteriological order. The engineering analog must respect this scope: not 'morality in general' but 'the operational discipline of a structured order'.

literal_meaning: Whenever there is decline of dharma, Bharata, and rise of adharma, then I manifest myself.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The most famous verse of chapter 4. Krishna names the cyclical pattern: when dharma declines (glāniḥ) and adharma rises, he manifests (sṛjāmi ātmānam). The verb sṛjāmi is striking — usually 'I create,' here applied reflexively (I manifest myself). Prabhupada explicitly notes that sṛjāmi here cannot mean 'create' since the Lord's form is eternal; it means 'manifest' in the sense of self-disposing appearance. The verse is the doctrinal centerpiece of the avatar block.
